nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing hill streams and rivers draining the Western Ghats; clear, well-oxygenated water over rocky and sandy substrates with riparian vegetation

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of aquarium foods. Offer high-quality flake or micro-pellets as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, and mosquito larvae. Vegetable matter (blanched spinach, spirulina-based foods) rounds out the diet and promotes good condition. Feed 2–3 times daily in small amounts.

goodWith: other medium barbs (e.g., Denisonii barb, Filament barb)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Dawkinsia arulius; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: A large, active schooling barb endemic to the Western Ghats of southern India. Males develop spectacular elongated dorsal-fin filaments in breeding condition. Best kept in groups of 8+ in a spacious, well-oxygenated river-biotope tank. IUCN Vulnerable due to restricted endemic range.
careNotes:
The Arulius Barb (Dawkinsia arulius) is a striking, active fish endemic to fast-flowing rivers and hill streams of the Western Ghats in Tamil Nadu and Karnataka, India. It was formerly placed in Puntius and then Systomus before Pethiyagoda et al. (2012) erected the genus Dawkinsia to accommodate this clade of South Asian barbs, with D. arulius as one of the type species. It is closely related to the Filament Barb (D. filamentosa) and can be confused with it in the trade; D. arulius typically has a more posterior dorsal spot and shorter adult filaments at comparable sizes.

A group of at least 8 specimens is strongly recommended — smaller groups result in increased intra-specific aggression and stressed subordinate fish. A tank of 200 L or more with a minimum footprint of 120 cm is advisable to provide sufficient swimming space for a shoal. Strong, efficient filtration and high oxygenation are important, reflecting the turbulent, well-oxygenated hill-stream habitat. Moderate to high flow via powerheads or spray bars is beneficial.

Decor should mimic a rocky river bed: smooth river stones, fine gravel or sand, and driftwood with robust plants anchored to hardscape (Java fern, Anubias). Avoid very soft, acidic water — the Western Ghats region includes significant limestone geology that imparts moderate hardness to many draining rivers, and the species tolerates moderately hard, slightly alkaline conditions well, although neutral to slightly acidic water is also acceptable.

The Arulius Barb is an enthusiastic fin-nipper when kept in undersized groups or with slow, long-finned tankmates; adequate group size greatly reduces this behaviour. Males are noticeably slimmer and develop elongated ray extensions on the dorsal fin — sometimes 2–3 rays extending well beyond the fin margin — particularly when in breeding condition. Females are deeper-bodied. The species is listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List owing to its restricted endemic range and ongoing habitat degradation from agricultural runoff, dam construction, and sand mining in the Western Ghats.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer. Conditioning a separate group with live and frozen foods will prompt spawning, which occurs among fine-leaved plants or over a spawning mop. Males display intensely to females, with dorsal filaments fully extended. Eggs are adhesive and hatch in 24–36 hours at 24–26°C. Parents and other fish will eat eggs and fry — a breeding net or dedicated tank is advisable. Fry are free-swimming within 3–5 days and accept infusoria and micro-foods initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp and finely crushed flake as they grow. Breeding in captivity has been achieved but is not commonplace in the hobby.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dawkinsia arulius
primary.notes: Primary reference. Provided taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Western Ghats, Tamil Nadu/Karnataka, India), adult size (to ~12 cm), water parameters, minimum group size, tank size recommendations, and notes on the 2012 reclassification to Dawkinsia.

additional.name: FishBase — Dawkinsia arulius
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, adult size (TL ~12 cm), temperature range (20–26°C), native range (peninsular India, Western Ghats rivers), diet (omnivore), and IUCN Vulnerable status. Confirmed order Cypriniformes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Arulius Barb
additional.notes: Trade-focused care overview. Confirmed group-size recommendation (6–8+), tank size (55 gal / ~208 L minimum for a shoal), feeding guidance (variety of flake, pellet, and frozen foods), and temperament notes (semi-aggressive fin-nipper in small groups).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Barbs of the Western Ghats
additional.notes: Background on Western Ghats barb biotope, water chemistry (moderate hardness, near-neutral pH), discussion of Dawkinsia reclassification (Pethiyagoda et al. 2012), and husbandry notes covering oxygenation and flow requirements. Noted confusion with D. filamentosa in trade.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Dawkinsia arulius
additional.notes: Confirms IUCN Vulnerable status; endemic to Western Ghats rivers in Tamil Nadu and Karnataka; threats include habitat degradation from agriculture, dam construction, and sand mining. Range map reviewed.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; breeding achievable in a species tank with fine-leaved plants or spawning mop. Parents will eat eggs; remove adults after spawning. Fry accept infusoria and micro-foods initially.
